title: Cultural & Social Anthropology
subtitle: Kinship, ritual, meaning & the diversity of human cultures
methodology: METHODOLOGY · Every entry anchors to a landmark study, ethnographic fieldwork, paper or theoretical text. The hint explains the idea in plain language; the note names the primary publication and flags replication status (later re-study or re-analysis), revisions and known controversies. Studies and effects carry a replicability badge — 🟢 robust · 🟡 nuanced or reduced · 🔴 failed or severely contested. Map pins mark the location where the fieldwork was conducted.

categories.label: Theories & Models
categories.subtitle: The frameworks that organise the study of culture, kinship and meaning

entries.label: Cultural Relativism
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Every culture must be understood on its own terms — by its own history, values and internal logic — rather than ranked against a Western yardstick of 'civilisation'. Boas argued that apparent differences in intelligence or custom reflect historical accident and environment, not innate racial hierarchy, directly attacking the unilinear evolutionism popular in 19th-century anthropology.
entries.note: Boas, F. (1896). 'The Limitations of the Comparative Method of Anthropology.' Science, 4(103), 901–908. Boas's students (Kroeber, Benedict, Mead) extended relativism into the dominant paradigm of American anthropology by the 1930s. Caveat: taken to an extreme, relativism can make cross-cultural ethical critique (e.g. of harmful practices) difficult to ground — a tension anthropologists still debate.

entries.label: Structural Functionalism
entries.loc: London School of Economics, London, England
entries.hint: A society's customs, rituals and institutions exist because they serve a function — typically maintaining social cohesion and equilibrium — much as organs serve functions within a living body. Radcliffe-Brown and Malinowski used this lens to explain why seemingly odd practices (elaborate mourning rites, ceremonial exchange) are not irrational survivals but active mechanisms holding the social order together.
entries.note: Radcliffe-Brown, A. R. (1922). 'The Andaman Islanders.' Cambridge University Press, laid out the structural-functionalist programme; Malinowski's functionalism (needs-based, individual-centred) diverged from Radcliffe-Brown's structural version (society-centred). Later critiqued by practice theorists (Bourdieu, Ortner) as too static — it explains why institutions persist but struggles to explain historical change or conflict within a society.

entries.label: Interpretive Anthropology & Thick Description
entries.hint: Culture is not a set of rules to be catalogued but a web of meaning to be read, like a text — the anthropologist's job is 'thick description': recording not just what people do, but what they and their community understand the act to mean. A wink, Geertz noted, can be a twitch, a flirtation, or a parody, and only the layered social context tells you which.
entries.note: Geertz, C. (1973). 'Thick Description: Toward an Interpretive Theory of Culture,' in The Interpretation of Cultures. Basic Books. Hugely influential in shifting anthropology from a scientific-explanatory register toward a humanistic-interpretive one. Critics (e.g., Crapanzano, V. 1986, 'Hermes' Dilemma,' in Writing Culture) argued interpretive anthropology offers no way to adjudicate between competing readings of the same culture, raising an unresolved question about whether its claims are falsifiable.

entries.label: Structuralism
entries.loc: École Pratique des Hautes Études, Paris, France
entries.hint: Beneath the surface diversity of myths, kinship rules and rituals lie universal deep structures of the human mind, built from binary oppositions (raw/cooked, nature/culture, male/female). Lévi-Strauss argued that comparing myths and marriage systems across cultures reveals the same underlying combinatorial logic, much as linguists find shared grammatical structures beneath different languages.
entries.note: Lévi-Strauss, C. (1949). 'Les Structures élémentaires de la parenté' (The Elementary Structures of Kinship). Presses Universitaires de France. Extended in Lévi-Strauss's four-volume 'Mythologiques' (1964–71). Critiqued for being difficult to falsify — a skilled structuralist can find a binary opposition in almost any myth — and for underplaying historical change and individual agency in favour of timeless mental structures.

entries.label: Linguistic Relativity (Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis)
entries.hint: The grammar and vocabulary of a person's language subtly shape how they perceive and categorise the world — speakers of languages with different spatial, temporal or classificatory systems may literally notice and remember different things. Whorf's strong claim (language determines thought) is now rejected, but a weaker version (language influences habitual attention) has real empirical support.
entries.note: Whorf, B. L. (1940). 'Science and Linguistics.' MIT Technology Review, 42, 229–231, 247–248, built on Sapir's earlier linguistic-relativity remarks. The strong 'linguistic determinism' reading was widely rejected by the 1960s–70s (e.g. universalist colour-term research by Berlin & Kay 1969). A weaker relativity has been empirically supported since, e.g. by Lucy's Yucatec Maya classifier study (st10) — see the Debates section for how this pendulum has swung across a century.

entries.label: Alliance Theory
entries.loc: École Pratique des Hautes Études, Paris, France
entries.hint: Kinship systems are best explained not by tracing descent (who is related to whom by blood) but by marriage exchange — societies enforce rules about who one must marry outside one's own group (exogamy), and this circulation of spouses between groups is what binds a society together, echoing Mauss's logic of gift exchange. Lévi-Strauss's alliance theory directly rivalled the British descent-theory school.
entries.note: Lévi-Strauss, C. (1949). 'Les Structures élémentaires de la parenté.' Presses Universitaires de France. Rivalled by descent theory (Fortes, M. 1949, 'The Web of Kinship Among the Tasallensi,' Oxford University Press; Radcliffe-Brown's lineage model). Neither theory alone explains every kinship system studied since — most anthropologists now treat alliance and descent as complementary lenses rather than competing universal laws.

entries.label: Rites of Passage & Liminality
entries.loc: École Pratique des Hautes Études, Paris, France
entries.hint: Life transitions — birth, puberty, marriage, death — are marked across cultures by a common three-stage ritual structure: separation from ordinary social status, a liminal in-between phase where normal rules are suspended, and reincorporation into a new status. Turner later showed the liminal phase often produces 'communitas' — an intense, equal, temporary bond among initiates that ordinary hierarchical society doesn't allow.
entries.note: Van Gennep, A. (1909). 'Les Rites de passage.' Émile Nourry, Paris. Extended by Turner, V. (1969). 'The Ritual Process: Structure and Anti-Structure.' Aldine. The tripartite model has been confirmed across an enormous range of cultures' rites, though some anthropologists note it fits initiation rituals better than it fits everyday, non-ceremonial transitions, and that not every liminal phase produces communitas.

entries.label: Gift Theory & the Norm of Reciprocity
entries.hint: A gift is never truly free — every gift carries an obligation to give, to receive, and to reciprocate, and this three-part obligation, not market exchange, is what binds archaic and small-scale societies together. Mauss showed that objects exchanged in ceremonies like the Kula ring or the potlatch carry the giver's identity with them, making the exchange as much social and spiritual as economic.
entries.note: Mauss, M. (1925). 'Essai sur le don' (The Gift). Année Sociologique. Drew heavily on Malinowski's Kula ring data (st1) and Boas's potlatch accounts. The theory has been broadly confirmed across gift economies studied since, though later economic anthropologists (e.g. Marshall Sahlins) refined it by showing reciprocity norms vary systematically with social distance — closer kin observe looser, more generalised reciprocity than distant trading partners.

entries.label: Cultural Materialism & Cultural Ecology
entries.hint: Material conditions — how a society gets its food, tools and energy from its environment — determine its social structure, kinship rules and even its religious beliefs, not the reverse. Steward's cultural ecology and Harris's later cultural materialism explain seemingly bizarre customs (a sacred, uneatable cow in India) as rational adaptations once the underlying material constraints (plough oxen, milk, dung fuel) are accounted for.
entries.note: Steward, J. H. (1955). 'Theory of Culture Change: The Methodology of Multilinear Evolution.' University of Illinois Press. Extended by Harris, M. (1974). 'Cows, Pigs, Wars, and Witches.' Random House. Critics (symbolic/interpretive anthropologists) argue cultural materialism reduces belief and meaning to a byproduct of economics, and Harris's specific explanations (e.g. of Yanomami warfare, st7) remain contested against rival, non-materialist accounts of the same data.

entries.label: Anthropology's Critique of Colonial Contact
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: The 'traditional' small-scale societies anthropologists studied were never isolated, timeless survivals — they were already deeply entangled with European colonialism, global trade and slavery long before the ethnographer arrived. Wolf argued that treating these societies as pristine, self-contained units erased the very history (conquest, forced labour, resource extraction) that had shaped them, and that anthropology needed to write people back into world history.
entries.note: Wolf, E. R. (1982). 'Europe and the People Without History.' University of California Press. Part of a broader 1980s reflexive turn (see also Asad, T. ed. 1973, 'Anthropology and the Colonial Encounter') that forced the discipline to reckon with its own historical entanglement with colonial administration. The critique reshaped how later fieldwork (e.g. among the Yanomami, st7) is framed and reported, though debate continues over how much any outside researcher can escape that entanglement.

categories.label: Landmark Studies
categories.subtitle: The defining experiments — and exactly what they found

entries.label: Malinowski's Kula Ring (Trobriand Islands)
entries.loc: Kiriwina Island, Trobriand Islands, Papua New Guinea
entries.hint: Trobriand Islanders undertake long, dangerous canoe voyages between islands purely to exchange ceremonial shell valuables — red necklaces (soulava) travelling one way, white armshells (mwali) the other — that have no practical use and are often given away again almost immediately. Malinowski showed this Kula ring was nonetheless the backbone of Trobriand political alliance, status and inter-island trade in ordinary goods.
entries.note: Malinowski, B. (1922). 'Argonauts of the Western Pacific.' Routledge & Kegan Paul, London — the founding text of economic anthropology and modern participant-observation fieldwork. Weiner, A. (1976). 'Women of Value, Men of Renown: New Perspectives in Trobriand Exchange.' University of Texas Press, restudied the same islands and showed Malinowski's male-centred account had omitted a parallel, women-controlled exchange system (banana-leaf bundles central to mortuary ritual), revising the picture of Trobriand political economy as more gender-balanced than he portrayed.

entries.label: Mead's Coming of Age in Samoa
entries.hint: Mead argued that Samoan adolescents passed through puberty without the emotional turmoil, sexual anxiety or rebellion long assumed to be a universal, biological feature of the teenage years — evidence that adolescent 'storm and stress' is culturally produced, not hard-wired. The claim became one of the most cited arguments for cultural over biological determinism in the nature-nurture debate.
entries.note: Mead, M. (1928). 'Coming of Age in Samoa: A Psychological Study of Primitive Youth for Western Civilisation.' William Morrow, New York. Freeman, D. (1983). 'Margaret Mead and Samoa: The Making and Unmaking of an Anthropological Myth.' Harvard University Press, argued Mead was misled by informants and that Samoan adolescence in fact involved real conflict and restriction — sparking anthropology's most public 20th-century controversy. Later scholars (Shankman, P. 2009, 'The Trashing of Margaret Mead') defended parts of Mead's account and criticised Freeman's own methodology, so the dispute remains genuinely unresolved.

entries.label: Evans-Pritchard's The Nuer
entries.hint: The Nuer, cattle-herding pastoralists of the South Sudanese swamplands, maintained political order across tens of thousands of people with no chiefs, courts or police — instead, a 'segmentary lineage system' let any two kin groups unite against a more distant threat and split apart again once it passed, producing order through shifting, balanced opposition rather than centralised authority.
entries.note: Evans-Pritchard, E. E. (1940). 'The Nuer: A Description of the Modes of Livelihood and Political Institutions of a Nilotic People.' Oxford: Clarendon Press. Restudied fifty years later by Hutchinson, S. (1996). 'Nuer Dilemmas: Coping with Money, War, and the State.' University of California Press, which documented profound change from a cattle-based to a money-and-war economy but largely confirmed Evans-Pritchard's original segmentary-lineage description as an accurate account of its own historical moment.

entries.label: Boas's Cranial Plasticity Study
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Boas measured the head shape (cranial index) of roughly 13,000 European immigrants and their American-born children and found significant differences between parents and children raised in the new environment — direct evidence that a supposedly fixed racial trait was in fact plastic and environmentally responsive, undercutting contemporary race science's claim that cranial form was a stable marker of biological type.
entries.note: Boas, F. (1912). 'Changes in Bodily Form of Descendants of Immigrants.' American Anthropologist, 14(3), 530–562. Sparks, C. S. & Jantz, R. L. (2002). 'A Reassessment of Human Cranial Plasticity: Boas Revisited.' PNAS, 99(23), 14636–14639, reanalysed the same data and argued it showed strong genetic — not environmental — determination. Gravlee, C. C., Bernard, H. R. & Leonard, W. R. (2003). 'Heredity, Environment, and Cranial Form: A Reanalysis of Boas's Immigrant Data.' American Anthropologist, 105(1), 125–138, found the Sparks & Jantz reanalysis statistically flawed and that Boas's original plasticity conclusion held up — the dueling-reanalysis dispute is itself a well-known case study in how the same historical dataset can be read to opposite conclusions.

entries.label: Radcliffe-Brown's Andaman Islanders
entries.hint: Radcliffe-Brown documented the elaborate ceremonial life of the Andaman Islanders — mourning rites, initiation ordeals, food taboos — and argued each custom, however strange it looked to an outsider, functioned to express and reinforce the value the community placed on social solidarity itself, making ritual a kind of collective self-affirmation rather than a superstitious relic.
entries.note: Radcliffe-Brown, A. R. (1922). 'The Andaman Islanders.' Cambridge University Press — the founding ethnographic monograph of British structural-functionalism. The descriptive account of Andamanese ritual life has held up well; his specific functionalist interpretation (that ritual exists purely to encode social solidarity) was later challenged by symbolic and practice-oriented anthropologists as too neatly systemic, underplaying conflict, individual variation and historical change within Andamanese society.

entries.label: Geertz's Balinese Cockfight
entries.hint: Balinese men stage lavish, illegal cockfights and bet heavily on them despite the fights having no material payoff worth the risk — Geertz argued the cockfight is really 'deep play,' a dramatized story Balinese society tells itself about status, masculinity and prestige, readable like a text once you understand the symbolic stakes riding on each match.
entries.note: Geertz, C. (1972). 'Deep Play: Notes on the Balinese Cockfight.' Daedalus, 101(1), 1–37 — the paradigm case for interpretive/symbolic anthropology's method of 'thick description.' Critics (e.g., Crapanzano, V. 1986, 'Hermes' Dilemma,' in Writing Culture, eds. Clifford & Marcus) argued Geertz offers no way to check his interpretation against a rival reading of the same event, an unresolved methodological tension that still divides interpretive anthropology from more empirically falsifiable approaches.

entries.label: Chagnon's Yanomami Fieldwork
entries.hint: Chagnon depicted the Yanomami as 'the fierce people,' a society organised around chronic intervillage raiding, and argued men who had killed (unokai) achieved higher status and more wives and children — evidence, he claimed, that reproductive competition drove tribal warfare. The claim, the fieldwork methods behind it, and the ethics of the research itself became the subject of one of anthropology's fiercest and most public disputes.
entries.note: Chagnon, N. A. (1968). 'Yanomamö: The Fierce People.' Holt, Rinehart & Winston. Tierney, P. (2000). 'Darkness in El Dorado: How Scientists and Journalists Devastated the Amazon.' W. W. Norton, alleged Chagnon's presence and gift-giving had itself inflamed village conflict and that his violence data were exaggerated, and accused a collaborating geneticist of worsening a measles epidemic. A 2005 American Anthropological Association review vacated its own earlier critical task-force report after finding the measles allegations unsubstantiated, though concerns about fieldwork ethics and informed consent were never fully resolved — the case remains a live example of how entangled scientific claims and research ethics can become.

entries.label: Shostak's Nisa: Life of a !Kung Woman
entries.hint: Shostak recorded the life story of Nisa, a Ju/'hoansi (!Kung San) woman, in her own words — childhood, marriages, childbirth, desire and grief — giving anthropology a rare first-person female account from a foraging society at a time when most ethnography of hunter-gatherers was written by, and largely about, men.
entries.note: Shostak, M. (1981). 'Nisa: The Life and Words of a !Kung Woman.' Harvard University Press. Shostak's own follow-up, 'Return to Nisa' (2000, Harvard University Press), found Nisa's life had grown harder amid rapid social and economic change in the intervening two decades — a contextualising update rather than a contradiction of the original life history, and a reminder that even a single-subject account is a snapshot of a particular historical moment, not a timeless portrait.

entries.label: Gough's Nayar Marriage Study
entries.hint: Among the traditional Nayar of Kerala, a woman could have several recognised 'visiting husbands' who held no rights or duties toward her children, who instead were raised within her own matrilineal household (taravad) — a kinship arrangement that broke the assumption, common across anthropology at the time, that marriage universally implies co-residence or exclusive economic and sexual rights.
entries.note: Gough, K. (1959). 'The Nayar and the Definition of Marriage.' Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute, 89(1), 23–34. Gough's account relied partly on elderly informants' memories of a system already declining under colonial-era land reform by the time of her fieldwork, and later scholars (e.g. Fuller, C. J. 1976, 'The Nayars Today,' Cambridge University Press) documented meaningful variation across Nayar subcastes — the core kinship pattern is well attested, but its uniformity across the whole Nayar population is less certain than early citations implied.

entries.label: Lucy's Yucatec Maya Cognition Study
entries.hint: Yucatec Maya grammar, unlike English, does not require marking most nouns for number and instead uses classifiers that sort objects by material or shape. Lucy showed that Yucatec Maya speakers and English speakers, given the same nonverbal sorting task, grouped objects differently in ways that tracked exactly this grammatical difference — a rare controlled empirical test giving real, if modest, support to linguistic relativity.
entries.note: Lucy, J. A. (1992). 'Grammatical Categories and Cognition: A Case Study of the Linguistic Relativity Hypothesis.' Cambridge University Press. Later research (surveyed in Lucy & Gaskins and in Lera Boroditsky's subsequent colour, space and time studies through the 2000s–2010s) found supporting evidence in some cognitive domains but weaker or contested effects in others, so the strength and universality of the Whorfian effect remains an actively debated question in cognitive science, not a settled result.

entries.label: Lee's !Kung 'Original Affluent Society' Study
entries.hint: Lee tallied how many hours Dobe Ju/'hoansi foragers actually spent gathering and hunting food in the Kalahari Desert and found it was only about 15–20 hours a week — enough for nutritional adequacy with time to spare, contradicting the assumption that hunter-gatherers lived in constant subsistence crisis. The finding became the empirical anchor for Sahlins's provocative 'original affluent society' thesis.
entries.note: Lee, R. B. (1968). 'What Hunters Do for a Living, or, How to Make Out on Scarce Resources,' in Lee, R. B. & DeVore, I. (eds.), 'Man the Hunter.' Aldine. Popularised in Sahlins, M. (1972). 'Stone Age Economics.' Aldine-Atherton. Later critiques (e.g. Sackett, R. 1996, 'Time, Energy, and the Indolent Savage') argued Lee's original tally excluded substantial time spent on food processing, tool-making and childcare, meaningfully revising the 'affluence' claim upward in total labour, though the core finding — that foraging is not the desperate, all-consuming struggle once assumed — has largely held.

categories.label: Effects & Phenomena
categories.subtitle: Named, recurring patterns in cultural and social organisation

entries.label: Cargo Cults
entries.hint: Across parts of Melanesia, new religious movements arose that ritually mimicked the practices of colonial cargo delivery — building mock airstrips, control towers and radios from bamboo and coconut — in the belief that correctly performing these rites would cause ancestral spirits to deliver Western manufactured goods. The John Frum movement on Tanna, Vanuatu, which still holds an annual ceremony, is the most famous and long-running example.
entries.note: First systematically documented by Worsley, P. (1957). 'The Trumpet Shall Sound: A Study of Cargo Cults in Melanesia.' MacGibbon & Kee. Independently recorded across many separate Melanesian islands (Vanuatu, Papua New Guinea, Fiji) rather than a single society, making it one of anthropology's most widely replicated named phenomena. Later scholars (Lindstrom, L. 1993, 'Cargo Cult') cautioned the term itself was partly a colonial construction that flattened very different local movements into one exoticised label.

entries.label: Avunculocal Emphasis (Mother's Brother Role)
entries.loc: University of Cape Town, Cape Town, South Africa
entries.hint: In many matrilineal societies, a child's biological father has comparatively little formal authority over them — instead, it is the mother's brother who holds disciplinary authority, inheritance obligations and ritual responsibility, because descent and property pass through the maternal line. Radcliffe-Brown showed this 'avuncular' emphasis recurs across unrelated matrilineal societies on different continents.
entries.note: Radcliffe-Brown, A. R. (1924). 'The Mother's Brother in South Africa.' South African Journal of Science, 21, 542–555. Confirmed across matrilineal societies studied since, including the Trobriand Islands (Malinowski, st1) and the Ashanti of West Africa. The strength of the mother's-brother role varies by specific society — ceremonial in some, fully displacing the father's authority in others — so the pattern is robust in direction but variable in degree.

entries.label: Potlatch Rivalry
entries.hint: Among Pacific Northwest Coast peoples such as the Kwakwaka'wakw, chiefs competed for status not by accumulating wealth but by ceremonially giving it away, or even destroying it outright — canoes, blankets, copper shields — in front of rival hosts, whose prestige demanded they eventually reciprocate with an even more lavish potlatch of their own.
entries.note: First documented by Boas, F. (1897). 'The Social Organization and the Secret Societies of the Kwakiutl Indians.' Report of the U.S. National Museum. Analysed theoretically by Mauss (th8) as a paradigm case of gift-based social obligation. The Canadian government banned the potlatch from 1885–1951, partly out of a documented misreading of it as wasteful; the practice persisted underground and was revived openly once the ban lifted, evidence of how central it remained to Kwakwaka'wakw social structure.

entries.label: Culture-Bound Syndromes (Amok)
entries.hint: In parts of Southeast Asia, 'amok' names a recognised pattern in which a person who has withdrawn and brooded after an insult or loss suddenly erupts into indiscriminate violent rage, often followed by exhaustion or amnesia — a locally named behavioural syndrome that doesn't map cleanly onto any single Western psychiatric category.
entries.note: Described from early colonial-era accounts and analysed anthropologically by Murphy, H. B. M. (1972). 'History and the Evolution of Syndromes: The Striking Case of Latah and Amok,' in Psychopathology: Contributions from the Social, Behavioral, and Biological Sciences. Wiley. Whether 'culture-bound syndromes' are genuinely distinct conditions or local idioms for universal psychiatric states remains contested — DSM-5 (2013) replaced the category with the broader, less essentialising 'cultural concepts of distress,' reflecting an unresolved debate.

entries.label: Couvade Syndrome
entries.hint: In many cultures, expectant or new fathers undergo ritual restrictions, or even physical symptoms mimicking labour and childbirth, timed to their partner's pregnancy and delivery — a pattern found among societies as geographically separated as Amazonian South America, parts of Africa, and the Basque Country of Europe.
entries.note: Comparative surveys include Munroe, R. L., Munroe, R. H. & Whiting, J. W. M. (1973). 'The Couvade: A Psychological Analysis.' Ethos, 1(1), 30–74, which found the custom recurs independently across many unrelated cultures rather than spreading from one origin. A related, medically documented phenomenon — expectant fathers reporting genuine physical symptoms — is now studied separately in psychology as 'Couvade Syndrome,' distinct from but clearly related to the ritual practice anthropologists first named.

entries.label: Big Man System
entries.hint: Across highland Papua New Guinea and much of Melanesia, political leadership is typically achieved rather than inherited: a 'big man' builds authority entirely through personal generosity, organising elaborate feasts and gift exchanges that place rivals in his debt, and can lose that authority just as fast if he stops delivering — a sharp contrast to the inherited chieftainship found elsewhere in the Pacific.
entries.note: Sahlins, M. (1963). 'Poor Man, Rich Man, Big Man, Chief: Political Types in Melanesia and Polynesia.' Comparative Studies in Society and History, 5(3), 285–303, contrasted the achieved Melanesian 'big man' with the inherited Polynesian 'chief.' The pattern has been confirmed across many separate Melanesian societies, though later fieldworkers (e.g. Andrew Strathern among the Mount Hagen peoples) documented considerable local variation in how big-man status is built and lost.

entries.label: Linguistic Taboo & Word Avoidance
entries.hint: Many languages require speakers to avoid ordinary words in certain social contexts — most strikingly, 'mother-in-law languages' found in Australian Aboriginal communities, where a person must use an entirely separate vocabulary in the presence of specific in-laws, and death commonly triggers a temporary ban on using the deceased's name or any word that sounds like it.
entries.note: Documented in Dixon, R. M. W. (1971). 'A Method of Semantic Description,' and later 'The Languages of Australia' (1980), Cambridge University Press, based on fieldwork with Dyirbal and other Australian languages. Similar name-avoidance-after-death customs are independently attested across unrelated language families in Africa and the Americas, suggesting the underlying social logic recurs even where the specific linguistic mechanism differs.

entries.label: Witchcraft Accusation Patterns
entries.hint: Across many small-scale societies, accusations of witchcraft cluster predictably around specific social fault lines — co-wives in polygynous households, ambitious neighbours, or kin disputing inheritance — rather than occurring randomly, suggesting witchcraft belief functions partly as a socially legible way of explaining and managing misfortune and interpersonal jealousy.
entries.note: Evans-Pritchard, E. E. (1937). 'Witchcraft, Oracles and Magic Among the Azande.' Oxford: Clarendon Press, first showed witchcraft accusation was a coherent, internally rational system of explaining misfortune rather than mere superstition. The clustering pattern has been confirmed in many later studies across Africa and elsewhere, though anthropologists still debate how much is a universal feature of small, tightly bound communities versus specific to particular kinship structures.

categories.label: Key Concepts
categories.subtitle: The building blocks and vocabulary every student must command

entries.label: Ethnocentrism
entries.hint: The tendency to judge other cultures using the standards, values and categories of one's own culture as if they were universal — treating one's own way of life as the natural, correct baseline against which everything else appears strange, primitive or wrong. Cultural relativism was developed largely as anthropology's methodological antidote to this default bias.
entries.note: Sumner, W. G. (1906). 'Folkways: A Study of the Sociological Importance of Usages, Manners, Customs, Mores, and Morals.' Ginn and Co., coined the term. Distinct from simple cultural pride or preference — ethnocentrism specifically involves treating one's own norms as an unmarked, objective standard rather than one perspective among many.

entries.label: Emic vs Etic Distinction
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: An 'emic' account describes a culture in its own members' terms and categories — what distinctions matter to them, in their own words — while an 'etic' account uses categories and measures imposed by the outside analyst for cross-cultural comparison. Good ethnography usually needs both: the emic view to understand meaning from the inside, the etic view to compare findings across different societies.
entries.note: Pike, K. L. (1954). 'Language in Relation to a Unified Theory of the Structure of Human Behavior.' Summer Institute of Linguistics, adapted from the linguistic terms 'phonemic' and 'phonetic.' Popularised in anthropology by Marvin Harris's cultural materialism (th9), which argued etic, materially-grounded explanations should take priority over purely emic, native-category ones — a methodological stance not every anthropologist shares.

entries.label: Unilineal Descent Groups
entries.hint: Many societies organise kinship, inheritance and political membership around descent traced through only one parent's line — patrilineal (through the father) or matrilineal (through the mother) — creating clearly bounded lineages and clans that structure marriage rules, land rights and political alliance, distinct from the bilateral kinship reckoning (both parents' sides count equally) familiar in most Western societies.
entries.note: Systematised in Fortes, M. & Evans-Pritchard, E. E. (eds.) (1940). 'African Political Systems.' Oxford University Press, based on extensive African fieldwork. Lévi-Strauss's alliance theory (th6) offered a rival account of the same kinship data, arguing marriage-exchange rules, not descent tracing, were the more fundamental organising principle — the two frameworks are now generally treated as complementary lenses.

entries.label: Taboo
entries.hint: A taboo marks certain persons, objects, words or actions as forbidden or dangerously charged — not because breaking the rule causes visible practical harm, but because it violates the community's sense of proper order, often carrying a belief in automatic supernatural or social consequence for the violator.
entries.note: The term entered anthropology from Polynesian 'tapu' via Captain Cook's 18th-century voyage accounts; theorised functionally by Radcliffe-Brown, A. R. (1939). 'Taboo' (Henry Myers Lecture). Cambridge University Press, who argued taboos encode and reinforce a society's core values by making their violation feel dangerous rather than merely disapproved of.

entries.label: Totemism
entries.hint: In many societies, clans or lineages identify symbolically with a particular animal, plant or natural object — their totem — treating it as an emblem of group identity, often with associated taboos (such as not eating one's totem species) and myths explaining the original bond between the group and its totem.
entries.note: Analysed at length in Durkheim, E. (1912). 'Les Formes élémentaires de la vie religieuse' (drawing on Australian Aboriginal Arunta data) and reinterpreted structurally by Lévi-Strauss, C. (1962). 'Le Totémisme aujourd'hui.' Presses Universitaires de France, who argued totemic classification is really a way of using natural differences between animal species to think about social differences between clans, not evidence of primitive confusion between humans and animals.

entries.label: Syncretism
entries.hint: When two belief systems come into sustained contact — most often through colonisation, slavery or migration — practitioners frequently blend elements of both into a new hybrid tradition rather than one simply replacing the other, as seen in Haitian Vodou or Cuban Santería, which fuse West African deities with Catholic saints under conditions of forced conversion.
entries.note: Extensively documented in Herskovits, M. J. (1937). 'Life in a Haitian Valley.' Alfred A. Knopf, and 'The Myth of the Negro Past' (1941), Harper & Brothers, which traced specific African religious retentions within New World syncretic traditions. Syncretism is now understood as an ongoing process, not a one-time historical event — many syncretic religions continue actively blending and reinterpreting elements today.

entries.label: Ethnogenesis
entries.hint: New ethnic or cultural identities can form — not just persist from time immemorial — when groups are thrown together or reshaped by conquest, forced migration, slavery or state-building, forging a shared identity, language or tradition that did not exist in that form before the disruption.
entries.note: Framework developed from Barth, F. (ed.) (1969). 'Ethnic Groups and Boundaries: The Social Organization of Culture Difference.' Universitetsforlaget, which argued ethnic identity is maintained by the boundary between groups, not by a fixed cultural content inside it — meaning boundaries, and the identities they mark, can and do form anew under the right historical pressures.

entries.label: Habitus
entries.loc: École des Hautes Études en Sciences Sociales, Paris, France
entries.hint: People carry with them a durable set of largely unconscious dispositions — ways of moving, tastes, gut reactions to situations — absorbed from their upbringing and social position, which then shape how they act without their having to consciously calculate each choice. Bourdieu argued this 'habitus' is how social structure gets reproduced inside individual bodies and minds, generation after generation.
entries.note: Bourdieu, P. (1972). 'Esquisse d'une théorie de la pratique' (Outline of a Theory of Practice). Librairie Droz. Developed further in Bourdieu's later sociological work on education and class (see the Sociology & Social Structures folio's 'Cultural Capital & Social Reproduction'). Critics note the concept can be hard to test directly, since almost any observed behaviour can be explained after the fact as an expression of habitus.

categories.label: Methods & Approaches
categories.subtitle: The tools and paradigms that generate the field's evidence

entries.label: Participant Observation
entries.loc: London School of Economics, London, England
entries.hint: The anthropologist lives among the people they study for an extended period — a year or more — learning the local language, participating in daily and ceremonial life, and recording observations as they happen, rather than relying on brief visits, interpreters, or secondhand colonial reports. Malinowski's Trobriand fieldwork set the template that made this the discipline's defining method.
entries.note: Malinowski, B. (1922). 'Argonauts of the Western Pacific.' Routledge & Kegan Paul, London — the methodological introduction is as influential as the ethnographic content. The method remains the discipline's gold standard, though later reflexive anthropologists (see the Malinowski Diary debate) noted that immersion does not eliminate the fieldworker's own biases and social position, which shape what gets recorded and how.

entries.label: Genealogical Method
entries.hint: Rivers developed a systematic technique for charting a community's full kinship network by asking each person to name and classify every relative by genealogical position, then cross-checking accounts across informants — turning kinship, previously described only impressionistically, into precise, comparable diagrams that could reveal a society's underlying descent and marriage rules.
entries.note: Rivers, W. H. R. (1900). 'A Genealogical Method of Collecting Social and Vital Statistics.' The Journal of the Royal Anthropological Institute, 30, 74–82, developed during the 1898 Cambridge Anthropological Expedition to the Torres Strait. The technique became standard equipment for kinship studies such as Evans-Pritchard's Nuer research (st3) and remains in use, refined into modern kinship-diagramming software.

entries.label: Salvage Ethnography
entries.loc: Bureau of American Ethnology, Washington, D.C.
entries.hint: Believing many Indigenous North American cultures were rapidly disappearing under colonisation, disease and forced assimilation, Boas and his students raced to document languages, myths, technologies and social organisation from surviving elders before that knowledge was lost — producing an enormous, if urgency-driven, ethnographic and linguistic record.
entries.note: Boas, F. (1907). 'Some Principles of Museum Administration.' Science, 25(650), 921–933, laid out the urgent documentation rationale; the method was carried out extensively by Boas and his students through the Bureau of American Ethnology and Columbia University for several decades afterward. The method preserved genuinely irreplaceable knowledge, but later critics noted its 'vanishing culture' framing sometimes ignored living communities' ongoing adaptation and survival, treating cultures as museum specimens rather than continuing, changing societies — a framing many descendant communities have since actively pushed back against.

entries.label: Cross-Cultural Survey Method (HRAF)
entries.hint: Rather than studying one culture deeply, this method systematically codes standardised variables (marriage rules, subsistence type, political complexity) across hundreds of societies from existing ethnographies, letting researchers statistically test whether a given custom correlates with other features of a society worldwide.
entries.note: Murdock, G. P. (1949) built the Human Relations Area Files (HRAF) at Yale University into a standardised, indexed cross-cultural database, later formalised into the Standard Cross-Cultural Sample (Murdock, G. P. & White, D. R. 1969, 'Standard Cross-Cultural Sample.' Ethnology, 8(4), 329–369). Critics note the method depends entirely on the quality and comparability of the original ethnographies being coded, and can flatten deep cultural context into checkbox variables.

entries.label: Linguistic Fieldwork & Elicitation
entries.loc: Bureau of American Ethnology, Washington, D.C.
entries.hint: To document a language with no prior grammar or dictionary, linguistic anthropologists systematically elicit words, sentences and narratives from native speakers, working out the sound system, grammar rules and semantic categories from scratch — essential both for endangered-language preservation and for testing claims about how a language's structure shapes its speakers' thought.
entries.note: Codified in Boas, F. (1911). 'Handbook of American Indian Languages,' Introduction. Smithsonian Institution — a founding methodological statement for descriptive linguistics as practiced within anthropology. The method underlies later empirical tests of linguistic relativity such as Lucy's Yucatec Maya study (st10), which depended on precise elicitation of grammatical categories before any cognitive testing could begin.

entries.label: Life History Method
entries.hint: Instead of describing a culture through its general patterns and institutions, this method records one person's life story in their own words — childhood, relationships, turning points — treating a single richly documented life as a window onto how broader cultural rules are actually lived, negotiated and sometimes broken by real individuals.
entries.note: Exemplified by Shostak, M. (1981). 'Nisa: The Life and Words of a !Kung Woman.' Harvard University Press (st8). The method trades the generalisability of large-sample surveys for depth and voice, and is especially valued for surfacing perspectives — often women's, children's, or other less-documented viewpoints — that broader institutional ethnography can miss entirely.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: Franz Boas
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Boas founded American academic anthropology and gave it its defining ethic: judge no culture by another's standards. His statistical debunking of fixed racial cranial types and his insistence on rigorous fieldwork over armchair theorising trained a generation (Kroeber, Benedict, Mead, Hurston) that carried cultural relativism into the discipline's mainstream.
entries.note: Boas, F. (1911). 'The Mind of Primitive Man.' Macmillan, synthesised his argument against biological racial hierarchy for a general audience. See also his 1896 relativism paper (th1) and 1912 cranial plasticity study (st4). Widely regarded as the founder of the 'four-field' American anthropology tradition (cultural, linguistic, biological, archaeological).

entries.label: Bronisław Malinowski
entries.loc: London School of Economics, London, England
entries.hint: Stranded in the Trobriand Islands by the outbreak of World War I, Malinowski turned an accident of circumstance into the founding template of modern fieldwork — long-term immersion, local language, daily participation — and used it to document the Kula ring, functionalist theory's landmark case study.
entries.note: Malinowski, B. (1922). 'Argonauts of the Western Pacific.' Routledge & Kegan Paul (st1). His posthumously published private field diary (1967) revealed considerable private hostility toward the Trobriand Islanders he studied, sparking a lasting debate about the gap between public ethnographic authority and a fieldworker's private attitudes.

entries.label: Margaret Mead
entries.hint: Mead brought anthropology to a mass American audience, arguing across a prolific career that supposedly universal, biologically fixed patterns — adolescent turmoil, rigid gender roles — were in fact culturally variable, using fieldwork in Samoa and New Guinea as her evidence.
entries.note: Mead, M. (1928). 'Coming of Age in Samoa.' William Morrow (st2). Her account was later disputed by Derek Freeman (1983), sparking one of anthropology's most public controversies; later scholarship (Shankman, P. 2009) defended significant parts of her work while acknowledging its limitations — her broader influence in popularising cultural over biological determinism remains undisputed regardless of how the Samoa dispute is resolved.

entries.label: E. E. Evans-Pritchard
entries.hint: Evans-Pritchard showed that societies without chiefs, courts or police could still maintain a coherent, describable political order, and that beliefs Westerners dismissed as superstition — like Azande witchcraft — formed a logically consistent system for explaining misfortune once understood on its own terms.
entries.note: Evans-Pritchard, E. E. (1937). 'Witchcraft, Oracles and Magic Among the Azande' and (1940) 'The Nuer.' Oxford: Clarendon Press (st3, ef8). His work bridged Radcliffe-Brown's structural functionalism and a more historically and symbolically attentive anthropology, influencing the later interpretive turn associated with Geertz.

entries.label: A. R. Radcliffe-Brown
entries.loc: University of Cape Town, Cape Town, South Africa
entries.hint: Radcliffe-Brown founded British structural-functionalism, arguing anthropology should be a comparative natural science of society — treating kinship systems, rituals and institutions as parts of a functioning social structure whose logic could be studied with the same rigour as any other natural system.
entries.note: Radcliffe-Brown, A. R. (1922). 'The Andaman Islanders' and (1924) 'The Mother's Brother in South Africa' (th2, st5, ef2). His structural-functionalist programme dominated British social anthropology for decades before being challenged by practice theory and interpretive anthropology from the 1970s onward.

entries.label: Claude Lévi-Strauss
entries.loc: École Pratique des Hautes Études, Paris, France
entries.hint: Lévi-Strauss argued that beneath the bewildering surface variety of the world's kinship systems and myths lies a shared, universal architecture of the human mind, organised around binary oppositions — turning anthropology's oldest question (why do cultures differ so much?) into a search for the deep structures that make them, underneath, alike.
entries.note: Lévi-Strauss, C. (1949). 'Les Structures élémentaires de la parenté' and the four-volume 'Mythologiques' (1964–71) (th4, th6). Founder of structural anthropology, hugely influential across the humanities and social sciences well beyond anthropology itself, though later critiqued for underplaying history, power and individual agency.

entries.label: Clifford Geertz
entries.hint: Geertz turned anthropology's centre of gravity from explaining cultures like biological systems toward reading them like texts — arguing that the anthropologist's job is 'thick description,' interpreting the layered meanings a community itself gives to its own symbols and practices, from a cockfight to a religious ceremony.
entries.note: Geertz, C. (1973). 'The Interpretation of Cultures.' Basic Books, and 'Deep Play: Notes on the Balinese Cockfight' (1972) (th3, st6). His interpretive turn reshaped anthropological writing across the 1970s–80s, though critics (Crapanzano and others in the 'Writing Culture' movement) pushed the reflexive critique of ethnographic authority even further than Geertz himself had.

entries.label: Marcel Mauss
entries.hint: Mauss showed that gift-giving in small-scale societies is never truly free — every gift carries a threefold social obligation to give, receive and reciprocate — reframing what looked like disconnected customs (the Kula ring, the potlatch) as expressions of one deep, cross-cultural logic of exchange and obligation.
entries.note: Mauss, M. (1925). 'Essai sur le don' (The Gift). Année Sociologique (th8, ef3). Durkheim's nephew and intellectual heir, Mauss's gift theory became foundational to economic anthropology and directly shaped later work by Sahlins, Lévi-Strauss and Bourdieu on exchange and symbolic capital.

entries.label: Marvin Harris
entries.loc: Columbia University, New York, New York
entries.hint: Harris argued that a society's seemingly irrational or purely symbolic customs — a sacred, uneatable cow in India, a taboo on pork in the Middle East — are usually rational adaptations to underlying material and ecological constraints, once you account for the practical costs and benefits the culture's own members may not consciously recognise.
entries.note: Harris, M. (1974). 'Cows, Pigs, Wars, and Witches: The Riddles of Culture.' Random House (th9). His cultural materialism directly rivalled Chagnon's sociobiological explanation of Yanomami warfare (st7) and Geertz's interpretive method, arguing material infrastructure, not meaning or reproductive strategy, should carry primary explanatory weight.

entries.label: Eric Wolf
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: Wolf argued anthropology had long studied 'traditional' societies as if they were timeless, isolated survivals, when in fact European colonialism, global trade and slavery had already reshaped nearly every society anthropologists ever studied — forcing the discipline to write these 'peoples without history' back into a connected global past.
entries.note: Wolf, E. R. (1982). 'Europe and the People Without History.' University of California Press (th10). Part of a broader 1970s–80s critical reassessment of anthropology's own entanglement with colonial power, which reshaped how later contested fieldwork — including Chagnon's among the Yanomami (st7) — has been interpreted and debated.

entries.label: Napoleon Chagnon
entries.loc: University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan
entries.hint: Chagnon's decades of fieldwork among the Yanomami produced some of anthropology's most detailed demographic and genealogical data on a small-scale society, and his sociobiological argument — that violence conferred reproductive advantage — made him simultaneously one of the field's most cited and most fiercely contested figures.
entries.note: Chagnon, N. A. (1968). 'Yanomamö: The Fierce People.' Holt, Rinehart & Winston (st7). Tierney's 'Darkness in El Dorado' (2000) triggered a formal AAA task-force review, later vacated in 2005 after key allegations were found unsubstantiated — leaving Chagnon's data widely used but his fieldwork ethics and interpretive framework still actively debated.

entries.label: Zora Neale Hurston
entries.loc: Barnard College, New York, New York
entries.hint: Trained by Boas, Hurston combined a novelist's ear for voice with anthropological rigour to document African American folklore in the U.S. South and Vodou and folk religion in Haiti and Jamaica, at a time when almost no Black scholars were given the standing to conduct or publish this kind of fieldwork.
entries.note: Hurston, Z. N. (1935). 'Mules and Men.' J. B. Lippincott, and (1938). 'Tell My Horse: Voodoo and Life in Haiti and Jamaica.' J. B. Lippincott (co6). Long under-recognised within academic anthropology relative to her literary fame, her fieldwork on syncretic Afro-Caribbean religion is now considered foundational, and her career is frequently cited in discussions of race and representation within the discipline's own history.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: Mead vs Freeman: Was Samoan Adolescence Free of Storm and Stress?
entries.hint: Mead's 1928 claim that Samoan girls passed through adolescence without Western-style turmoil became a foundational argument for cultural over biological determinism — until Derek Freeman spent decades gathering counter-evidence arguing Samoan society was in fact sexually restrictive and prone to violence, and that Mead's teenage informants had likely been joking with her. The clash became the most public fight in the discipline's history.
entries.note: Freeman, D. (1983). 'Margaret Mead and Samoa: The Making and Unmaking of an Anthropological Myth.' Harvard University Press, laid out the critique in detail. Shankman, P. (2009). 'The Trashing of Margaret Mead.' University of Wisconsin Press, defended Mead's methods and argued Freeman's own evidence was selectively presented. Later Samoan-American scholars have argued both sides sometimes spoke past actual, more complicated Samoan realities — the dispute has never been definitively settled either way.

entries.label: Chagnon vs Ferguson/Harris: What Explains Yanomami Warfare?
entries.hint: Chagnon argued Yanomami violence was driven by reproductive competition — men who had killed gained more wives and children. Critics countered the pattern reflected recent disruption from Western contact (steel tools, disease, missionary and state pressure reshaping local politics) rather than a timeless biological pattern, and that Chagnon's own fieldwork presence may have intensified the very conflict he was measuring.
entries.note: Chagnon, N. A. (1988). 'Life Histories, Blood Revenge, and Warfare in a Tribal Population.' Science, 239(4843), 985–992, made the reproductive-success case. Ferguson, R. B. (1995). 'Yanomami Warfare: A Political History.' School of American Research Press, and Harris's cultural-materialist tradition (th9) argued a political-historical and material account fit the evidence better. Both sides agree the violence was real; they disagree sharply on its cause and on how much Chagnon's own presence shaped what he recorded.

entries.label: Cultural Relativism vs Universal Human Rights
entries.hint: If every culture's values must be understood on its own terms, can anthropology still condemn practices as human rights violations, or does that condemnation itself impose one culture's standard on another? The discipline's own founding professional body initially sided with relativism against a universal declaration, then reversed itself decades later.
entries.note: American Anthropological Association (1947). 'Statement on Human Rights.' American Anthropologist, 49(4), 539–543, argued against the drafting UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights on relativist grounds. The AAA reversed course in its 1999 'Declaration on Anthropology and Human Rights,' affirming a role in defending human rights while still insisting on cultural context — a compromise that hasn't fully resolved the underlying tension.

entries.label: Is Anthropology a Science?
entries.hint: In 2010 the American Anthropological Association quietly edited its own long-range plan, removing the word 'science' from its mission description in favour of language about 'public understanding' — a small wording change that exploded into a public argument over whether anthropology is fundamentally an empirical science, a humanistic discipline, or an uneasy combination of both.
entries.note: The 2010 revision and ensuing controversy were widely covered, e.g. Wade, N. (2010). 'Anthropology a Science? Statement Deepens a Rift.' The New York Times, December 9, 2010. The dispute traces back at least to Geertz's interpretive turn (fg7) against more scientific, comparative traditions; the AAA partially restored 'science' language after backlash, but the underlying methodological split remains unresolved and recurring.

entries.label: Linguistic Relativity: How Strong Is the Whorfian Effect?
entries.hint: Whorf's strong claim that language determines thought was widely rejected after Berlin and Kay found color terms emerge in a predictable universal sequence across unrelated languages — but decades later, more careful experiments revived a weaker version of his idea, showing the pendulum has swung back and forth for over a century.
entries.note: Berlin, B. & Kay, P. (1969). 'Basic Color Terms: Their Universality and Evolution.' University of California Press, delivered the strongest universalist counter-evidence. Lucy, J. (1992, st10) and later work by Boroditsky and colleagues through the 2000s–2010s found real, replicated relativity effects in other grammatical domains. Current consensus is moderate: language measurably shapes habitual cognition in some domains, without Whorf's original strong determinism.

entries.label: Malinowski's Private Diary vs His Public Ethnography
entries.hint: Malinowski's own field diary, published decades after his death, revealed private frustration, boredom and outright racist contempt toward the very Trobriand Islanders his published ethnography treated with careful, respectful attention — forcing anthropology to confront how much a finished ethnography's polished authority can mask a fieldworker's messier private reality.
entries.note: Malinowski, B. (1967). 'A Diary in the Strict Sense of the Term.' Harcourt, Brace & World, published by his widow. Geertz, C. (1988). 'Works and Lives: The Anthropologist as Author.' Stanford University Press, used the diary's shock to help launch the wider 1980s reflexive turn questioning ethnographic authority. Defenders note private frustration during isolating fieldwork doesn't necessarily invalidate the resulting ethnography, but the episode permanently changed how the discipline discusses the fieldworker's own subjectivity.

entries.label: Sahlins vs Obeyesekere: How Did Hawaiians Understand Captain Cook?
entries.hint: Sahlins argued Hawaiians initially welcomed Captain Cook as an incarnation of the god Lono, returning exactly on cue with the ritual calendar, explaining both his triumphant welcome and his sudden killing when he returned off-schedule. Obeyesekere countered this was really a Western fantasy projecting 'natives' as prelogical mystics, when Hawaiians in fact reasoned about the foreign captain in practical political terms.
entries.note: Sahlins, M. (1985). 'Islands of History.' University of Chicago Press, made the ritual-calendar argument. Obeyesekere, G. (1992). 'The Apotheosis of Captain Cook.' Princeton University Press, argued the 'natives thought he was a god' narrative was itself a colonial myth. Sahlins, M. (1995). 'How "Natives" Think.' University of Chicago Press, replied with additional Hawaiian-language source evidence. The dispute became a landmark case study in interpreting other societies' meanings without exoticising or flattening them.

entries.label: How Exceptional Was Nayar Marriage?
entries.hint: Gough's account of Nayar 'visiting husbands' and matrilineal households became a textbook case forcing anthropologists to redefine marriage itself — but later fieldworkers found Gough had relied partly on elderly informants recalling an already-declining, colonial-era system, and documented real variation across different Nayar subcastes that early citations tended to flatten into one uniform pattern.
entries.note: Gough, K. (1959, st9). Fuller, C. J. (1976). 'The Nayars Today.' Cambridge University Press, documented significant subcaste variation and further social change. The core finding — that some historical Nayar groups had kinship arrangements not fitting a universal definition of marriage — is not seriously disputed, but exactly how widespread and uniform that arrangement was remains a live question of historical reconstruction.

entries.label: Are There Human Cultural Universals?
entries.loc: University of California, Santa Barbara, California
entries.hint: Decades of anthropological emphasis on cultural variation led many to assume there were essentially no meaningful human universals — until Brown compiled a long list of traits (facial expressions for basic emotions, incest taboos, some form of music and myth) found in literally every documented human society, challenging the field to explain why these particular patterns recur everywhere despite otherwise vast cultural diversity.
entries.note: Brown, D. E. (1991). 'Human Universals.' McGraw-Hill, compiled the most systematic list. Relativists and particularists in the tradition traced to Boas (th1) countered that many of Brown's 'universals' are so loosely defined that they risk being true by definition, or that superficial similarity can mask deep functional differences — a live methodological dispute about how strong a claim 'universal' can honestly support.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: Applied Development Anthropology
entries.hint: Anthropologists are now routinely embedded in international development projects — irrigation schemes, resettlement programs, public health campaigns — specifically to identify local social structures, land-tenure customs and kinship obligations that a purely technical or economic plan would otherwise overlook and inadvertently disrupt.
entries.note: Institutionalized through bodies like the Society for Applied Anthropology (founded 1941) and incorporated into World Bank and USAID project design from the 1970s onward, partly in response to earlier, anthropologically uninformed development failures. Critics (echoing Wolf's critique, th10) note applied anthropologists can still end up serving funding-agency priorities rather than the communities they nominally represent.

entries.label: Forensic Anthropology & Human Rights Investigation
entries.hint: Biological anthropologists' skeletal analysis skills — normally used to reconstruct age, sex and cause of death from ancient remains — have been redirected to identify the victims of state violence and mass atrocity, giving families answers and courts admissible evidence in cases that might otherwise remain permanently unresolved.
entries.note: The Argentine Forensic Anthropology Team (Equipo Argentino de Antropología Forense, EAAF), founded 1984, pioneered this application by exhuming and identifying victims of Argentina's 1976–83 military dictatorship. The same methods have since been used in Rwanda, the former Yugoslavia, and numerous other post-conflict investigations, now a standard tool of international criminal tribunals.

entries.label: Design Anthropology in Industry
entries.loc: Xerox Palo Alto Research Center, Palo Alto, California
entries.hint: Technology and consumer-goods companies now hire anthropologists to observe how people actually use products in their homes and workplaces — not just what they say they want in a survey — because ethnographic observation regularly reveals unspoken workarounds and needs that focus groups miss entirely.
entries.note: Pioneered in part by Suchman, L. (1987). 'Plans and Situated Actions.' Cambridge University Press, from her ethnographic work at Xerox PARC. Now standard practice at major technology firms (Intel's Genevieve Bell led one of the best-known corporate ethnography teams); anthropologists debate whether serving corporate product design compromises the discipline's traditional independence from the interests being studied.

entries.label: Language Revitalization Programs
entries.hint: Linguistic anthropology's documentation methods are now actively redeployed to reverse language loss rather than just record it — immersion preschools, adult classes and community media have measurably increased fluent speakers of languages that government policy had previously suppressed for generations.
entries.note: The Kōhanga Reo ('language nest') Māori-language immersion preschool movement, founded in New Zealand in 1982, became an internationally influential model, followed by similar Hawaiian-language immersion schools and numerous North American Indigenous-language revitalization programs. Outcomes vary by community resources and severity of prior loss, but the approach has demonstrably produced new fluent speakers.

entries.label: Repatriation of Sacred Objects and Human Remains
entries.hint: Museums and universities that had accumulated Indigenous human remains and sacred ceremonial objects, often collected without consent during the colonial and salvage-ethnography era, are now legally required in some countries to identify likely descendant communities and return those remains and objects on request.
entries.note: The U.S. Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (NAGPRA), signed 1990, required federally funded museums to inventory and, on request, repatriate Native American human remains and sacred objects. Comparable legislation followed in Australia and elsewhere. Implementation has been uneven and sometimes contested, but the law fundamentally reshaped the ethics of collections built up during the salvage-ethnography era (me3).

entries.label: Medical Anthropology in Global Health
entries.hint: Medical anthropologists working alongside public health practitioners have shown that treatment programs fail when they ignore local understandings of illness, stigma and poverty — and succeed when clinical care is redesigned around a community's actual social and economic constraints.
entries.note: Farmer, P. (1994). 'AIDS and Accusation: Haiti and the Geography of Blame.' University of California Press, and his subsequent work with the NGO Partners In Health, demonstrated that directly-observed, community-based tuberculosis and HIV treatment, redesigned around patients' actual living conditions, dramatically improved outcomes in Haiti's Central Plateau — a model since adopted by the World Health Organization and replicated in dozens of countries.
